headline: Copper King Marcus Daly Dies, 1900
body: Well now, folks, gather 'round and let me spin you a yarn about a man who knew a thing or two about digging for riches, and maybe a bit more about digging up trouble. Marcus Daly, they called him, though some might have preferred to call him a force of nature, or perhaps a particularly stubborn mule. He was one of Montana's famed Copper Kings, a title that sounds grand, don't it? Like something out of a fairy tale, if fairy tales involved dynamite, vast fortunes, and enough political wrangling to make a seasoned politician blush. This Daly, he was born in the Emerald Isle, County Cavan, back in '41, and by the tender age of fifteen, he'd decided that Ireland, bless its green heart, wasn't quite big enough for his ambitions. So he packed his hopes and dreams, and likely not much else, and set sail for the promised land of America. He landed in New York, a city that could swallow a man whole if he wasn't careful, but Daly, he had a knack for not being swallowed. He worked his way west, a telegraph messenger here, a miner there, learning the ropes of the earth's hidden treasures in places like California and the legendary Comstock Lode in Virginia City. He rubbed shoulders with the likes of John William Mackay and George Hearst, men who understood that the real gold wasn't always in the ground, but in the grit of the men who dug for it. It was in Butte, Montana, that Daly truly struck his claim, though not for the silver he was initially after. No, sir, the Anaconda mine, which he bought for a song and a dance, turned out to be sitting on a copper lode so rich it would make a miser weep with joy. And with that, the Anaconda Copper Mining Company was born, and Butte, a town that had been a rough-and-tumble silver camp, began its transformation into the "Richest Hill on Earth." Now, a man like Daly, with that much copper in his veins and that much ambition in his heart, was bound to make a few enemies. And his most famous, or infamous, adversary was none other than William A. Clark, another Copper King, though of a different stripe. Their rivalry, bless its cantankerous soul, was a spectacle to behold, a feud that simmered and boiled for decades, shaping the very landscape of Montana, both literally and politically. Some folks said it started over a bit of timber, others over water rights, and still others whispered about old-world grievances, the kind that cross oceans and generations. Whatever the spark, it ignited a blaze that consumed fortunes and reputations. They fought over mines, over railroads, and most famously, over a seat in the United States Senate. Clark, he wanted that Senate seat something fierce, and Daly, well, Daly was just as determined that Clark shouldn't have it. The newspapers of the day, they had a field day with it all, reporting on the accusations of bribery and the general shenanigans that went on. It was a grand old circus, with Montana as the main tent. Daly, he wasn't just about digging holes in the ground, mind you. He built the city of Anaconda, a company town, where his smelter belched smoke and prosperity, and he played a hand in making Helena the state capital, a decision that no doubt ruffled a few feathers in Butte. But even the mightiest of Copper Kings, with all their wealth and power, can't outrun the inevitable. On November 12, 1900, in a grand hotel in New York City, Marcus Daly, the man who had carved an empire out of the Montana earth, breathed his last. The True Republican, a newspaper of the time, reported, "Mr. Daly's death had been expected for weeks. He came home from Europe about the middle of September, and soon afterward was obliged to take to the bed from which he never again arose." It was Bright's disease, they said, complicated by a weak heart, a fitting end, perhaps, for a man whose heart had been so fiercely engaged in the battles of his life. He left behind a legacy as complex and enduring as the copper veins he unearthed, a testament to the raw, untamed spirit of the American West, and a reminder that even in the pursuit of riches, a good story, and a good fight, are never far behind.
